denial_letter_text:
Dear Member,

This letter concerns the prior authorization request your provider submitted for laparoscopic cholecystectomy to treat symptomatic cholelithiasis. After review under the UnitedHealthcare Medical Management Guidelines, the request is denied as not medically necessary. Determination date: May 20, 2026.

United has determined that a lower level of care is appropriate at this time and that laparoscopic cholecystectomy exceeds what is required. The documentation does not support that conservative outpatient management - including dietary modification and a trial of medical therapy - has been exhausted before surgery. The coverage criteria applied in this review reflect surgical outcomes data in patients ages 18 to 50.

Your request was received at 2:14 PM and this determination was issued at 2:17 PM on the same day, following review by an Optum clinical reviewer.

You or your provider may request, at no charge, a copy of the specific guideline and the documents relied upon in reaching this decision. If your provider wishes to discuss this determination, a peer-to-peer review with a United medical director may be arranged by calling the number on your member ID card.

You have the right to appeal. To file an internal appeal, you or your authorized representative must submit a written request within 180 days of the date of this letter, along with any additional clinical information. You may also request an external review by an independent review organization after the internal appeal.

Sincerely,
UnitedHealthcare Medical Management
clinical_context: The patient is a 62-year-old woman with a six-month history of recurrent biliary colic - episodic right-upper-quadrant pain radiating to the right scapula after fatty meals, with associated nausea and two urgent-care visits in the first quarter of 2026. Right-upper-quadrant ultrasound demonstrates multiple gallstones with gallbladder wall thickening to 5 mm and a positive sonographic Murphy sign, indicating gallbladder inflammation and a meaningful risk of progression to acute cholecystitis; her alkaline phosphatase was mildly elevated. She has already trialed dietary fat restriction over several months without resolution of her recurrent attacks. Her general surgeon documents that laparoscopic cholecystectomy is the definitive standard-of-care treatment for symptomatic cholelithiasis with these features and is not a higher level of care than required; continued conservative management risks an emergent presentation. The surgical outcomes data for patients ages 18 to 50 the insurer cites does not govern the operative indication for a 62-year-old with wall thickening and recurrent symptoms. The submission and denial were timestamped only three minutes apart, leaving no opportunity for individualized review of these records.

Procedural disclosures intact but clinical/factual vectors overwhelming.
